topics.title: Reactive Programming & Spring WebFlux — Mono, Flux & Backpressure
topics.description: Project Reactor's Mono and Flux types, the event-loop model that replaces thread-per-request, backpressure to prevent OOM under load, flatMap for parallel async calls, and Spring WebFlux controllers backed by Netty instead of Tomcat.
topics.story: Imagine you run a call centre. Traditional blocking Java is like hiring one agent per caller — agent sits on hold with the caller until the call ends, then picks up the next. With 500 concurrent callers you need 500 agents. Reactive is like a modern IVR switchboard: one operator handles hundreds of live lines, delegates tasks ('please hold while I check your account'), and gets notified the moment a result is ready. No agent sits idle. Spring WebFlux is that switchboard, and Mono/Flux are the message contracts — Mono carries one response, Flux streams many.
topics.analogy: Mono is a courier who delivers exactly one package (or nothing if the address doesn't exist). Flux is an Amazon conveyor belt — packages arrive one by one, and you chain processing stations: scan (map), reject damaged items (filter), split bundles (flatMap). The belt only moves as fast as the slowest station can process — that automatic speed adjustment is backpressure. If a downstream station sends demand('give me 10'), the belt emits exactly 10 and pauses.
topics.memoryAnchor:
Mono = 0 or 1 item async: like Optional but lazy
Flux = 0-to-N stream: like Stream but push-based
flatMap = map + merge: use for parallel async calls
subscribe() = terminal operator: nothing runs until you subscribe
boundedElastic = blocking adapter: wrap JDBC/file calls here
backpressure = demand signal: downstream controls upstream rate
topics.firstPrinciples:
Blocking I/O: thread calls network/DB, thread sleeps until response, thread wakes, thread handles next request. At 500 concurrent requests you need 500 threads, each ~1 MB stack = 500 MB just for thread stacks.

Event-loop model: 1 thread registers interest in I/O via OS selector (epoll/kqueue). OS notifies thread when data is ready. Thread processes the callback and re-registers for the next event. 4 CPU threads handle 10 000 concurrent connections — no sleeping threads, no wasted stacks.

Project Reactor builds this contract in Java: Flux<T> is a publisher that emits items. Operators (map, filter, flatMap) are assembly-line stages that transform the stream lazily. Nothing executes until a subscriber attaches. Each subscriber signals demand (request(n)) so producers never overwhelm consumers.
topics.bruteForce: Thread-per-request with blocking RestTemplate or JdbcTemplate — simple to write, hits Tomcat's default 200-thread limit at ~200 concurrent in-flight requests. Under load: HTTP 503 or queue pile-up.
topics.optimizedApproach: Spring WebFlux on Netty with Mono/Flux pipelines. Controllers return Mono<ResponseEntity<T>> or Flux<T>. Downstream HTTP calls use WebClient (fully non-blocking). Blocking operations (legacy JDBC) are isolated on Schedulers.boundedElastic() so they don't block the event loop. flatMap enables parallel fan-out across multiple services with a single thread.
topics.whenToUse: High-concurrency microservices: API gateways, notification services, real-time feeds, streaming endpoints (SSE/WebSocket). Calling multiple downstream services in parallel — flatMap collapses them into one stream. Avoid for simple CRUD apps — Spring MVC is easier to reason about and often faster for low-concurrency scenarios due to lower overhead.

examples.title: Spring WebFlux Controller with Mono & Flux, plus WebClient fan-out
examples.explanation: findById returns Mono<User> — exactly one item or an error. onStatus converts 4xx to a typed exception. timeout + retryWhen add resilience without blocking a thread. findAllActive returns Flux<User> — items stream one by one; the client can use SSE (text/event-stream) to consume them. buildProfile uses Mono.zip to run both HTTP calls concurrently then merge the results — two WebClient calls fire simultaneously on the same event-loop thread.
examples.realWorldUse: API gateway aggregating user, score, and subscription data in one endpoint. Instead of three serial blocking calls (3 × 200 ms = 600 ms), Mono.zip fires all three in parallel and returns in ~200 ms.

examples.title: Reactive pipeline operators: flatMap vs concatMap, error handling, Schedulers
examples.explanation: flatMap subscribes to all inner publishers concurrently (up to the concurrency cap) — use it when you want parallel execution and don't care about order. concatMap is strictly sequential — use it when downstream must receive items in input order. onBackpressureBuffer with DROP_OLDEST prevents the fast producer from OOM-ing the JVM. Schedulers.boundedElastic() is the correct scheduler for blocking I/O — it creates threads on demand up to a cap, preventing the event-loop thread from blocking.
examples.realWorldUse: Importing a large CSV: read line Flux, flatMap to async DB insert with concurrency=10, onErrorContinue to skip malformed rows, collect results at the end — all without spawning a thread per row.

problems.title: FluxPipeline — simulate a Reactor pipeline using Java Streams
problems.description:
Simulate the Reactor pipeline:
  Flux.range(1, 10)
      .filter(n -> n % 2 == 0)
      .map(n -> n * n)
      .collectList()

Use IntStream to replicate the behaviour. Print:
  Even squares: [list]
  Count: N
  Sum: total
problems.hint1: IntStream.rangeClosed is the Java Streams equivalent of Flux.range. Chain filter and map on it.
problems.hint2: Call .boxed() after the IntStream map to convert IntStream to Stream<Integer>, then .collect(Collectors.toList()).
problems.hint3: Build result list first, then print it. Sum = result.stream().mapToInt(Integer::intValue).sum(). The expected output is: Even squares: [4, 16, 36, 64, 100] / Count: 5 / Sum: 220.

problems.title: BackpressureBuffer — bounded queue with overflow tracking
problems.description:
Implement a BackpressureBuffer that holds up to `capacity` items. If the buffer is full when emit(value) is called, increment a dropped counter instead of adding the item. Provide a drain(n) method that removes and returns up to n items from the front.

Demo:
  capacity = 3, emit 1..5 → buffer holds [1,2,3], dropped=2
  drain(2) → [1, 2]
  emit(6), emit(7) → buffer becomes [3, 6, 7]

Print:
  Buffered: 3
  Dropped: 2
  Drained: [1, 2]
  Buffer after: [3, 6, 7]
problems.hint1: Use a LinkedList as the internal queue. emit() checks size vs capacity before offering.
problems.hint2: drain(n) loops n times calling poll() until the queue is empty, collecting results into a List.
problems.hint3: For 'Buffer after', wrap the internal LinkedList in new ArrayList<>(buffer) to get a list you can print with the right format. Expected: Buffered: 3 / Dropped: 2 / Drained: [1, 2] / Buffer after: [3, 6, 7].

problems.title: AsyncFanOut — parallel CompletableFuture fan-out then merge
problems.description:
Simulate Reactor's flatMap + Mono.zip pattern using CompletableFuture.

Given IDs [1, 2, 3], for each ID:
  - fetchUser(id) returns CompletableFuture<String> → "User-{id}"
  - fetchScore(id) returns CompletableFuture<Integer> → id * 100

Fan out both calls in parallel per ID using thenCombine, collect all results, sort them alphabetically, and print each on its own line followed by "Total: N".

Expected output:
  User-1:100
  User-2:200
  User-3:300
  Total: 3
problems.hint1: Map each id to a CompletableFuture<String> using fetchUser(id).thenCombine(fetchScore(id), (user, score) -> user + ":" + score). Collect into a List.
problems.hint2: Use CompletableFuture.allOf(futures.toArray(...)).join() to wait for all. Then map each future to its result with .join().
problems.hint3: After collecting results into a List<String>, call .stream().sorted().collect(Collectors.toList()) to ensure alphabetical order before printing. Expected: User-1:100 / User-2:200 / User-3:300 / Total: 3.
